w:r.w:t: Outreach is the professional service wing of

College of Social Sciences, founded in 1982 and registered as an NGO in 2002. The organization works with various segments of the community, including child and youth development, women empowerment, elderly care, community health, social impact assessment (SIA), natural resource management, environmental protection, and corporate social responsibility (CSR). Its primary focus is to enhance the well-being and improve the livelihood of individuals and communities.

One of the key projects for children is the Kaval initiative, which supports children in distress, while Kaval Plus specifically helps children who have been victims of various social issues. Both projects provide psychosocial support. In the sphere of women empowerment, the organization conducts multiple programs aimed at equipping women with skills and providing financial assistance through CSR funding. These initiatives encourage women to become self-reliant by engaging in employment or entrepreneurship.
Outreach established the first day care
, in 1996. Over the years, they have expanded to three additional

offer elderly citizens a chance to come together and participate in recreational activities such as yoga, meditation, leisure trips, health camps, awareness classes, and celebrations on significant days.

I reported to the office at 9 am, completed the registration process, and attended an orientation session led by Mr. Abhijith, the intern coordinator. He provided an overview of the organization’s operations and its various projects. As a social work trainee, I view this internship as an opportunity to explore the diverse challenges faced by communities and develop practical skills through collaboration with projects like Kaval, Kaval Plus, and Surekha. I hope to gain
valuable on-field experience in understanding and addressing community issues.
On the second day I reported at the
Outreach by 9 am. After marking the
I went out to Suraksha Migrant Project
for field work. I reported there by
am. The faculties at Surekha provide an orientation class about how they work among the migrants and which are the measures they took to prevent the spreading of Aid/
among the migrants and their family. The class continued till noon. And after the lunch

I was assigned to upload the data they collected from the migrants to their website. Following this work they also assigned me to complete an excel sheet which also contains the information about the migrants.
Understand the functioning of the agency - projects, activities, target population and
Understand in depth knowledge about the application of social work methods in dealing with individuals and groups.
To accompany them in their office work.
On my second day at Suraksha Migrant Project,

, I had the opportunity to observe how the office is well-organized and how they have made arrangements to spread awareness among migrants about HIV/AIDS prevention. I noticed that the staff are highly trained and knowledgeable, explaining their activities and services very clearly and effectively. The

orientation session gave me a deeper understanding of the challenges migrants face, especially regarding HIV/AIDS, and helped me learn how social work methods are applied in addressing sensitive issues. I was also assigned practical tasks such as uploading migrant data onto the website and completing an Excel sheet, which taught me the importance of proper documentation in social work. I learned that HIV/AIDS spreads through infected needles, from an infected mother to her child, or through unprotected sexual contact, and that the project focuses mainly on migrants from other states and truck drivers. The staff work actively to create awareness, encourage condom use, and promote safe sexual practices to prevent the spread of the disease. I also learned that the project covers areas like

, North Paravoor, and Ernakulam. Overall, the day was both informative and meaningful, giving me hands-on experience with documentation and a deeper insight into the agency’s functioning. I realized the importance of teamwork, accurate data management, and community outreach in achieving the goals of social work. This experience strengthened my understanding of the real-life application of social work and the need for active engagement with vulnerable communities.
On this day, the social work trainee reported at
Outreach at 9:00 am to take part in the planning and preparation for the ‘
’ summer camp, which is scheduled to be held on April 24th and 25th at HIHS School,

Social Work Department, is designed primarily for students enrolled in the scholarship scheme. The main objective of the camp is to equip students with life skills, transform them into responsible individuals, and help them strengthen their mental health and well-being.

The theme of the camp, “value-based living and mental health,” guided all the activities, games, and sessions that were planned throughout the day. The trainee, along with other interns, actively participated in group discussions and brainstorming sessions to develop meaningful and engaging activities. The interns divided themselves into three groups based on the school levels

School — and each group planned activities appropriate for their students. After the lunch break, the interns came together to prepare activities for common sessions that would energize and engage all participants during the camp. Later in the afternoon, the trainee joined the UP group to arrange materials and finalize preparations for sessions focusing on family and school relationships.
During the day, the trainee observed the enthusiastic participation and teamwork displayed by all interns, which highlighted how effective

collaborative efforts can be compared to working alone. It was also observed that while the module provided relevant session plans, some parts, such as the action songs, were outdated and might not appeal to today’s youth,
the need to adapt older materials to modern interests and expectations.

This experience offered several important learnings. The trainee gained practical skills in session planning, learned how to align activities with the camp theme, and developed creative ways to design engaging games. Importantly, the trainee also learned to critically evaluate the module, recognizing which parts were useful and which needed updating.

Overall, the day was productive and insightful. It provided a valuable opportunity to practice key social work skills such as teamwork, creativity, adaptability, and critical thinking. The hands-on preparation deepened the trainee’s understanding of the challenges involved in working with children and adolescents, especially when addressing topics like mental health and values. This experience will be a strong foundation for future fieldwork and professional growth.
On this day, the social work trainee reported at HIHS School,
’ summer camp. This camp, an initiative of

Social Work Department, was designed primarily for students enrolled in their scholarship scheme. The main aim of the camp was to equip, transform, and guide students to become responsible individuals while helping them address their mental health needs. Nearly 50 students, including those from the scholarship program, participated in the camp. The social work trainee played a key role in coordinating and conducting the camp in line with the theme “value-based living and mental health.”

The day began with preparations and registration, after which the camp formally started with an ice-breaking session led by the trainee. Following the inauguration, the students were divided into groups based on school levels—LP, UP, and HS. The trainee conducted sessions for

, focusing on themes of family and school. The group was further divided into two teams to promote teamwork, engagement, and a healthy sense of competition. Students earned points for their groups based on participation and performance, which helped sustain their enthusiasm throughout the session.

During the family session, the trainee emphasized the importance of family relationships, respect, and family budgeting. An activity was given where students listed wants and needs from their perspective, followed by a discussion on managing family income and the value of saving through
practices like maintaining a coin box. After the lunch break, the trainee energized participants with action songs. The camp concluded with a lively treasure hunt and group games.

The trainee observed the active participation and enthusiasm of most students, though a few showed signs of social withdrawal. Afternoon sessions presented challenges due to limited content, but these were managed through quick decision-making and teamwork. There was also a noticeable lack of coordination among the trainees during the afternoon, highlighting the need for better pre-camp planning.

The experience offered a practical platform to apply theoretical knowledge, develop flexibility, and build leadership and communication skills. The trainee also recognized the need for better preparation and improved strategies to engage students showing low social interaction.

The trainee gained valuable insights into planning and conducting a value-based, mental health-focused camp, strengthening leadership, teamwork, and adaptability skills while learning the importance of addressing individual needs within a group.
The camp effectively met its goals of promoting values and mental health awareness, though improvements are needed in afternoon content planning and team coordination.
Overall, the summer camp was a rewarding and enriching experience, providing significant professional learning and growth for the social work
The second day of the camp at

was dedicated to personal development and practical career guidance. We started the day with an interactive workshop on career planning, where students had the opportunity to share their dreams and aspirations. The session was designed to encourage self-reflection on their interests and how these could align with potential career paths. We provided valuable guidance on selecting the right educational courses and explored various career options, including both traditional and emerging fields. This session aimed to inspire students to think critically about their futures and make informed choices.

Following the career planning session, we transitioned into an essential workshop on personal safety, with a focus on understanding the concept of "good touch" and "bad touch." Through practical examples and real-life scenarios, we educated students about how to recognize inappropriate

and the steps to take in uncomfortable situations. The session was handled with care and sensitivity, creating a safe space for students to ask questions and express their concerns. This was a crucial part of the camp, as it empowered students to protect themselves and reinforced the importance of personal boundaries.

The afternoon was filled with vibrant cultural and recreational activities. Students participated in group games that encouraged cooperation, communication, and creativity. These games helped foster a sense of teamwork and provided a fun way for the students to engage with one another. The

highlight of the afternoon was the talent showcase, where students had the chance to perform songs, dances, and recite poems. This not only provided a platform for students to express their creativity but also strengthened the bond among them.

As the day concluded, students shared their feedback, expressing how much they enjoyed the mix of educational and recreational activities. The positive energy and active participation throughout the day made the second day of the camp a resounding success, leaving both the students and the camp organizers with a sense of
On the sixth day of fieldwork, I reported at the Suraksha Migrants Project of

Outreach in Perumbavoor by 9:30 am. The day began with an orientation session led by the faculty at Suraksha, who explained their work among the migrant population and the various measures they undertake to prevent the spread of HIV/AIDS among migrants and their families. This session offered a detailed understanding of their projects, target groups, and strategies. Later, I was assigned to complete an Excel sheet containing details of the migrants, a task I had previously carried out at the Suraksha

. After the lunch break, I continued with the data entry work. In the afternoon, we visited the nearby FSW (Female Sex Workers) office, where I received another informative orientation about their activities and interventions with female sex workers. The staff also provided a practical demonstration on the correct use of condoms, using a penis model, to explain safe sex practices. Observing the office setup and learning about their awareness activities helped me understand the importance of targeted interventions for high-risk groups. I noticed the professionalism and expertise of the staff, who were clear and thorough in explaining their work. The orientation helped me better understand the challenges faced by migrants in relation to HIV/AIDS prevention and the crucial role of social work interventions in this context. Managing the Excel documentation gave me practical experience in the importance of maintaining accurate records in social work practice. I learned that HIV/AIDS can spread through infected needles, sexual

contact, or from mother to child, and that migrants from various states, particularly those working in plywood factories and as truck drivers, are among the key target populations. The project’s efforts to raise awareness, promote condom use, and encourage safe sexual practices are essential to preventing transmission within families and communities. I also learned that Suraksha’s work covers areas like Perumbavoor,

. Overall, the fieldwork experience was both educational and meaningful, as it deepened my understanding of how social work agencies operate, the practical use of documentation, and the importance of combining community engagement with preventive health measures. The exposure to the FSW office further broadened my perspective on how targeted interventions can effectively support vulnerable populations and reduce the spread of HIV/AIDS.
day of fieldwork, the social work trainee reported to the Kripa Centre for Rehabilitation and Early Intervention, Aluva, at 9:

am. Kripa, established in 1981 by the CMI congregation, is a renowned institution dedicated to supporting individuals with disabilities through special education and vocational training programs. Over the years, it has provided training to around 750 differently-abled individuals in areas such as candle making, bookbinding, computer training, and animal husbandry, aiming to enhance their independence and promote their inclusion in society. Currently, 357 students aged between 16 and 30 are enrolled in a three-year vocational training program, and an additional
scholars attend the special school. The Early Intervention
at Kripa offers a wide range of therapies, including physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and

therapy, designed to address the developmental needs of younger children. After an initial discussion with Fr. Dibin, the manager of Kripa, the trainee, along with the other interns, was assigned to assist in the bookbinding section, where they helped prepare books intended for students affected by the Wayanad landslide disaster. The trainee participated actively in tasks such as folding papers, arranging the folded papers into sections, preparing the books for cutting, and stacking the final products. This hands-on experience provided a practical understanding of the various steps involved in the bookbinding
process, while also offering a chance to work alongside and support the differently-abled staff at Kripa.

Throughout the day, the social work trainee carefully observed how the bookbinding process was organized and monitored by the staff, many of whom were individuals with disabilities. It was particularly inspiring to witness their high level of expertise, commitment, and professionalism in carrying out their tasks. The supportive and collaborative atmosphere created by the management and staff contributed to an efficient workflow and also fostered a sense of shared purpose among the interns and employees. The trainee noted how adaptable and flexible the interns needed to be, adjusting quickly to the work environment and unfamiliar tasks, while also appreciating the patience, guidance, and encouragement offered by the staff. The experience reinforced the importance of teamwork, communication, and initiative in professional settings. It also highlighted the critical role of institutions like Kripa in creating opportunities for marginalized groups, promoting both their economic independence and social dignity.

Reflecting on the day’s experience, the trainee recognized how meaningful it was to contribute to a project that directly benefited a community in need, while also learning from the lived experiences of individuals with disabilities. The fieldwork at Kripa broadened the trainee’s understanding of inclusive vocational education and rehabilitative services, deepened their empathy, and strengthened their professional skills. It was a powerful reminder of the potential of social work to bridge gaps in society, empower vulnerable populations, and foster a more inclusive and compassionate world. Overall, the sixth day at Kripa was a rewarding and eye-opening experience that will have a lasting impact on the trainee’s future practice as a social worker.

camp was an engaging and impactful experience, thoughtfully designed to create a positive environment for the students while providing meaningful learning opportunities. The day began with a series of ice-breaking games aimed at building rapport among the students, fostering an inclusive atmosphere, and encouraging teamwork and communication. These activities were essential in helping the students feel comfortable with one another and prepared them for the rest of the day’s sessions. After successfully creating a friendly and cooperative atmosphere, the program moved into a career development session. This session was focused on helping students explore their interests, strengths, and aspirations, guiding them to think critically about potential career paths. The students were encouraged to reflect on their goals, understand the importance of perseverance, and discuss how to align their skills with future opportunities. The session was interactive and lively, with students sharing their thoughts, asking insightful questions, and showing genuine enthusiasm for the topic.

Following the career session, the focus shifted to environmental awareness, a crucial topic in today’s world. A workshop on waste management was conducted, during which students learned practical strategies for reducing, reusing, and recycling waste in their homes, schools, and communities. The session emphasized the importance of environmental responsibility and aimed to empower students to become agents of change in promoting sustainability.

The students responded positively to the session, showing interest in how they could apply these practices in their daily lives. In the afternoon, the students participated in a treasure hunt activity, designed to promote teamwork, problem-solving, and collaboration. This dynamic and engaging exercise required the students to work together, think critically, and communicate effectively in order to complete the tasks and locate the “treasure.” The treasure hunt not only served as a fun and energetic break from the more formal sessions but also reinforced the themes of teamwork and leadership introduced earlier in the day.

The camp concluded with a vibrant cultural performance segment, where students had the opportunity to showcase their talents through singing, dancing, and poetry recitation. This closing event provided a platform for self-expression and creativity, allowing the students to share their unique abilities and build confidence in front of their peers. The performances were met with enthusiasm and applause, creating a joyful and celebratory atmosphere that brought the day to a close on a high note. Throughout the day, the students demonstrated remarkable engagement, energy, and eagerness to participate, which contributed significantly to the camp’s success.
For the social work trainees, the first day of the

camp offered valuable learning experiences and opportunities for professional development. It allowed them to practice key social work skills such as communication, group facilitation, leadership, adaptability, and teamwork. The day’s activities not only enriched the students’ learning but also fostered a sense of community and belonging. Overall, the first day of the camp was a memorable and meaningful experience that combined education, entertainment, and empowerment, laying a strong foundation for the days to follow.
The second day of the camp at

was thoughtfully designed to focus on personal development and provide students with essential life skills that would benefit them in both their academic and personal lives. The day began with a highly engaging session on career planning, where students were guided through the process of making informed decisions about their education and future careers. During this session, we emphasized the importance of aligning their personal interests, strengths, and skills with their career choices. We also explored a wide range of professional fields, both traditional and emerging, to help broaden their understanding of the opportunities available to them. The students were actively involved in the discussion, eagerly sharing their aspirations and seeking advice on how to achieve their goals. Their curiosity and enthusiasm made the session interactive and meaningful, as they reflected on their ambitions and the steps required to pursue them.

Following the career session, we moved into one of the most crucial workshops of the camp, which addressed the topic of personal safety, specifically focusing on good touch and bad touch. This session was handled with great sensitivity and care, ensuring that students felt safe and comfortable while discussing such an important topic. We used simple explanations, relatable examples, and role-playing activities to help students understand how to recognize inappropriate
and how to respond or seek help if they encounter uncomfortable situations. The students were encouraged to express their concerns, ask questions, and share their thoughts, which fostered an open and
supportive learning environment. It was heartening to witness their active participation and their genuine interest in learning about how to keep themselves and their peers safe.

The afternoon was filled with cultural and recreational activities that allowed the students to relax, have fun, and strengthen their teamwork and leadership skills. A series of group games were organized to promote cooperation, communication, and problem-solving abilities among the participants. The energy and excitement were evident as students worked together, strategized, and cheered for one another throughout the games. In addition to the group activities, we hosted a talent showcase that provided students with the opportunity to present songs, skits, dances, and other artistic expressions. The variety and creativity of the performances reflected the unique talents of each participant and contributed to a lively and celebratory atmosphere.

To wrap up the day, we held a group reflection session where students were invited to share their key takeaways from the day’s activities. They spoke about the importance of making thoughtful career decisions, the value of understanding personal boundaries and safety, and the joy of expressing themselves creatively. Many students also expressed gratitude for the supportive environment created by the camp and noted how the experience had helped boost their confidence. Overall, the second day of the
camp was a resounding success, combining education, empowerment, and entertainment in a way that left a lasting impact on all participants.
The visit to the Elderly Day Care Centre at
, was an enriching and heartwarming experience that offered deep insights into the importance of elderly care and community support.
has been a pioneer in elderly care initiatives in Kerala, having started its first day care centre in 1996 and later expanding to three more centres, including
,” are designed to provide a safe, welcoming, and engaging environment where senior citizens can spend their days meaningfully, overcoming loneliness and finding a renewed sense of joy and purpose.
When we arrived at the centre in

, we were warmly welcomed by the staff and the elderly participants. The atmosphere was lively and cheerful, reflecting the centre’s focus on creating a positive and supportive environment for its members. The day began with a yoga and meditation session, where the elderly participants actively took part in simple exercises and breathing techniques guided by trained instructors. This session not only promotes physical health but also helps improve mental well-being by reducing stress and encouraging mindfulness.

Following the wellness activities, the participants engaged in a variety of recreational activities, including indoor games, singing, storytelling, and group discussions. It was inspiring to see how these activities foster a sense of community and belonging among the elderly, many of whom may otherwise face isolation at home. The centre also conducts awareness classes on important topics such as health care, nutrition, and legal rights, ensuring that the elderly remain informed and empowered. On the day of our visit, there was a discussion session where the participants reflected on their life experiences, shared their memories, and engaged in meaningful conversations with each other and the staff. This not only strengthens their social connections but also enhances their sense of self-worth and dignity.
One of the most remarkable aspects of the centre is the care and attention provided by the staff, who treat the elderly with utmost respect, patience, and

kindness. It was evident that the staff are not just professionals but are also deeply committed to ensuring that each beneficiary feels valued and supported. In addition to regular activities, the centre organizes leisure trips, health camps, festival celebrations, and observation of important days, all of which contribute to the holistic well-being of the elderly.
An interesting fact we learned is that the day care model initiated by

was later adopted by the Government of Kerala, which now runs similar centres across the state, recognizing the critical need for elderly-focused social spaces. This reflects the impact and sustainability of the model pioneered at
day of our fieldwork, we had the opportunity to visit Rewild
, a truly unique initiative dedicated to promoting sustainable agricultural practices through the principles of permaculture. This visit was
that offered deep insights into ecological farming and its importance in addressing some of the most pressing environmental challenges of our time.

Our day at Rewild began with an engaging orientation session led by Manoj Kumar I.B., fondly referred to as Manoj Sir, a respected expert in permaculture farming and the founder of the initiative. Manoj Sir introduced us to the core principles of permaculture, which emphasize the creation of agricultural ecosystems that closely mimic natural processes. The central idea behind permaculture is to design sustainable and self-sufficient farming systems that work harmoniously with nature, thereby reducing environmental harm and promoting long-term ecological balance.

During the orientation, Manoj Sir stressed the critical importance of involving young people in agriculture. He spoke passionately about the need to revive interest in farming among the younger generation and the role they can play in building a more sustainable future. This message was particularly inspiring, as it reminded us of the interconnectedness between human actions, the environment, and future generations.
Following the orientation, we participated in several practical demonstrations that enriched our understanding of sustainable farming techniques. We

learned about the importance of maintaining soil health, how to make compost using organic waste, and the role of plant diversity in enhancing ecosystem resilience. The hands-on component of the session was especially memorable. We took part in watering the plants using techniques that had been carefully demonstrated to us, ensuring that water was used efficiently and without wastage. This experience underscored the significance of even small, thoughtful actions in conserving precious natural resources.

Another important aspect of the session was the discussion on water conservation methods. We were introduced to practices like rainwater harvesting, which helps in maintaining soil moisture and reducing dependence on external water sources. These methods are not only effective in conserving water but are also crucial in making farms more resilient to climate variability.

The session also delved into the topic of community-based farming initiatives. We explored how such models contribute to local food security, strengthen local economies, and foster social cohesion. It was particularly interesting to reflect on how modern farming practices can benefit from integrating traditional knowledge systems, creating agricultural approaches that are both innovative and deeply rooted in cultural heritage.

was both educational and inspiring. It provided a unique opportunity to witness sustainable farming in action and to reflect on how these practices can be applied in our own lives. As a social work trainee, this experience deepened my appreciation for the link between environmental sustainability and social well-being. It has motivated me to think critically about promoting sustainable practices, not only in agricultural settings but also within the broader context of community development and social work.
day of our fieldwork, we visited the Erdely Day Care Centre at
, one of the four elderly day care centres run by
. This visit provided us with valuable insights into the Elderly Care Support programmes and the organization’s meaningful work in enhancing the lives of senior citizens.
has been a pioneer in elderly care, having initiated its first day care centre in 1996. Over the years, the programme has expanded to three additional centres—

—across Ernakulam District, Kerala. The main intention behind these centres is to provide senior citizens with a recreational platform where they can overcome loneliness, build social connections, and rejuvenate themselves in a supportive and joyful group atmosphere.

From the moment we arrived, we observed how the staff and volunteers engaged the elderly beneficiaries with the utmost respect, kindness, and care. The centre is designed to promote not just physical well-being but also emotional and psychological wellness, recognizing the importance of holistic care in later life. The trained professionals and volunteers ensure that the elderly experience joy, peace of mind, and a sense of dignity and self-esteem in all activities.

During our time at the centre, we participated in several ongoing activities. The elderly residents were engaged in recreational programs, including indoor games, singing, and storytelling. We also observed sessions of yoga and

meditation, which are part of the daily routine to promote both physical and mental health. It was heartwarming to witness the enthusiasm and active participation of the elderly, which reflected the positive impact of these programmes on their overall well-being.

One of the highlights of the day was the group discussion, where the elderly participants shared stories, memories, and reflections on life. These conversations were not only meaningful for the participants but also offered us a glimpse into their rich life experiences and the importance of creating spaces where their voices are heard and valued. Additionally, we learned about the various other activities organized by the centre, such as leisure trips, health camps, awareness classes, and the celebration of important days and festivals, all of which contribute to a lively and engaging environment.

A remarkable aspect of this initiative is that it served as a model for the Government of Kerala, which has since replicated such day care centres across the state. This underscores the significance and success of

Overall, the visit to Erdely Day Care was an eye-opening and inspiring experience. As a social work trainee, I gained a deeper understanding of the importance of elder care services in addressing the social, emotional, and physical needs of senior citizens. This experience has also reinforced my commitment to advocate for and contribute to community-based programmes that uphold the dignity and well-being of the elderly.
Field Work Activity at KRIPA Aluva

On the thirteenth day of our fieldwork, we continued our vocational training at KRIPA, Aluva, further building on the bookbinding skills we had been developing in previous sessions. This session focused on refining the techniques we had learned earlier and improving both the quality and efficiency of our work.

The day began with a brief review session, during which the trainers highlighted key points from the earlier trainings, such as precision in folding, gluing, and assembling materials. They emphasized the importance of consistency and careful attention to detail throughout the bookbinding process.

During the hands-on portion of the training, we practiced more advanced techniques aimed at strengthening the final product. We learned methods for improving spine strength, ensuring even page alignment, and achieving neat, professional-looking finishes. The trainers provided individualized guidance, helping each of us identify areas where we could improve our technique.

An important part of the day’s discussion focused on the social and economic value of vocational skills. The trainers explained how bookbinding, as a practical skill, can open up opportunities for income generation, especially for individuals in marginalized communities. High-quality bookbinding can also increase the durability of educational materials, making them more accessible and sustainable for schools, libraries, and community organizations.

Throughout the session, we worked collaboratively, helping one another and sharing tips we had picked up in earlier sessions. This teamwork not only improved the overall quality of the work but also fostered a sense of camaraderie and shared purpose. The atmosphere in the training room was encouraging and motivating, as everyone was focused on improving their skills and learning from each other.

We ended the day with a group reflection, where we shared our thoughts and takeaways from the training. Many of us expressed how much we had grown in confidence and skill over the course of the sessions. We also reflected on the importance of craftsmanship and the personal satisfaction that comes from creating something useful with our own hands. Most importantly, we discussed how vocational training can empower individuals and strengthen communities by promoting self-reliance and economic resilience.

Overall, the thirteenth day at KRIPA was another valuable experience that deepened our understanding of the role of vocational education in social development. It left us feeling inspired to apply the practical skills we’ve learned not only in future projects but also in our ongoing commitment to community service and empowerment.
Today marked the 14th day of my fieldwork at

, and it was a meaningful and productive experience. I began the day by working on the summary report, which involved compiling and documenting the activities and key events that have taken place so far in the outreach program. Preparing this report helped me reflect on the progress we have made and identify areas that need more attention or improvement. It also gave me the opportunity to develop my documentation and organizational skills.

After completing the report in the morning session, we had our lunch break. In the afternoon, I went to the ASK Centre to conduct the Spoken English class. The main aim of this class was to improve the students'

skills and boost their confidence. I engaged the students in activities like self-introductions, basic conversations, vocabulary practice, and pronunciation exercises. The students participated actively and showed a lot of enthusiasm, which made the session enjoyable and effective. I also made sure to provide encouragement and correct their mistakes gently, helping them feel more comfortable using English.

Later in the afternoon, I was responsible for arranging the Vedic Maths class. My role included preparing the classroom, ensuring that materials were ready, and helping organize the students. The Vedic Maths class focused on teaching simple and effective techniques for faster calculations, which are very useful for students in improving their mathematical abilities.

Overall, the day was a balanced mix of administrative work and hands-on engagement with the students. It was a valuable learning experience that helped me improve my time management, teaching, and organizational skills. I look forward to continuing to contribute to the outreach program in the coming days.
Today was the last day of my 15-day internship at
Outreach. It was a special and memorable day for me, as I looked back on all the things I learned and the
I was able to do during my time here.
In the morning, I worked on the

of my internship. This report included a review of all the activities I took part in over the past two weeks. I wrote about the work I did, such as preparing daily reports, helping with assisting with other tasks. Writing this report helped me understand how much I have grown and how many new things I have learned during this internship.

Outreach was full of feelings of happiness and thankfulness. This internship has helped me improve my skills in teaching, organizing, and communicating. It has also helped me understand the importance of helping others and working as a team. I am grateful to
Outreach for giving me this chance to learn and serve. I will always remember the lessons and the wonderful people I met during these 15 days.
